label: Statuses
title: Statuses
lead: Planned documentation for status effects and their gameplay hooks.
bodyHtml:
What statuses are for

Use CUCoreLib statuses when your mod wants new per-instance fields on a vanilla Body or Limb without editing the game's classes. The storage is backed by ConditionalWeakTable, but the supported consumer surface is the one-line extension call body.GetStatus<TStatus>() or limb.GetStatus<TStatus>().

Body body = PlayerCamera.main.body;
SunstrokeStatus status = body.GetStatus<SunstrokeStatus>();

status.ExposureSeconds += Time.deltaTime;

Define a body status with new fields

Body-first is the simplest pattern. Inherit from BodyStatus, add the fields your mod needs, and optionally add [StatusOptions] when you want a stable save key or want to disable saving for transient data.

[StatusOptions(Key = "com.yourname.sunstroke", SaveEnabled = false)]
      // The above ^ is optional, it defaults to being saved naturally without need for a key 
      public sealed class SunstrokeStatus : BodyStatus
      {
          public float ExposureSeconds;
          public float CoolingGraceSeconds;
          public bool WarnedPlayer;
      }

These fields are ordinary C# fields. Think of them as extra body data for your mod.

Read vanilla fields and update your own state in Update()

A common pattern is: read real vanilla body values, update your attached status, then write the gameplay effect back into other vanilla body fields. This keeps your custom logic self-contained while still using the real game simulation.

[HarmonyPatch(typeof(Body), "Update")]
public static class BodyUpdateStatusPatch
{
    private static void Postfix(Body __instance)
    {
       
        SunstrokeStatus status = __instance.GetStatus<SunstrokeStatus>();

        if (__instance.temperature > 39.2f)
        {
            status.ExposureSeconds += Time.deltaTime;
        }
            
        if (status.ExposureSeconds > 30f)
        {
            __instance.thirst = Mathf.Max(0f, __instance.thirst - Time.deltaTime * 1.5f);
            __instance.stamina = Mathf.Max(0f, __instance.stamina - Time.deltaTime * 2f);
        }
    }
}

Limb statuses use the same pattern

Per-limb state works the same way, just from LimbStatus and limb.GetStatus<T>(). Use this when your extra data truly belongs to one wound, one treatment timer, one custom fracture rule, or one custom infection system on a specific limb.

public sealed class FrostbiteStatus : LimbStatus
      {
          public float ExposureSeconds;
          public bool HasNerveDamage;
      }

      Limb foreleg = body.limbs[2];
      FrostbiteStatus frostbite = foreleg.GetStatus<FrostbiteStatus>();

When to use statuses instead of other storage

Use caseBest place
Per-body runtime fields that act like new vanilla body variablesBodyStatus
Per-limb runtime fields tied to one limb instanceLimbStatus
Registration-time metadata for a custom item/tile/building definitionCustomData on the registered definition
Large mod-wide systems not owned by one body/limb instanceYour own manager plus SaveRegistry provider if needed

Statuses are meant for instance-attached mutable state, not generic global storage.

Given that there will only ever be one Body, you technically can? But it's not great to do so regardless. :p

Moodles

A status can optionally provide player-facing UI through MoodleRegistry. If you want the shortest pattern, call MoodleRegistry.AddMoodle(...) directly from your update logic whenever the condition is active.

if (body.respiratoryRate < 50f)
{
    MoodleRegistry.AddMoodle(
        1,
        "hypoventilation",
        "Hypoventilation",
        "You're breathing unusually slowly.",
        important: true
    );
}

Use this when the status should show a normal moodle-like warning. For broader moodle behavior and icon expectations, see the Moodles page.
